title: Quick check: remove inbox rules and forwarding

questions.prompt: What three categories does this playbook step remove?

choices.label: Spam filters, signature blocks, and out-of-office replies.
choices.rationale: Those are not the named categories.

choices.label: Mailbox quotas, archive policies, and retention tags.
choices.rationale: Compliance-adjacent settings, not compromise artefacts.

choices.label: Inbox rules only.
choices.rationale: Incomplete. The lesson's whole point is the multi-mechanism shape.

questions.explanation: (ForwardingSmtpAddress/ForwardingAddress). Routing to hidden folders (with the existing routed mail noted). Three categories: inbox rules, mailbox-level forwarders, and hidden-folder routing with documentation. The multi-mechanism check is the discipline.

questions.prompt: "Removing the rule but not the forwarder" leaves what risk?

choices.label: The rule re-creates itself if the forwarder is left in place.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

choices.label: Removing the rule alone causes a portal error.
choices.rationale: Wrong on platform behaviour.

choices.label: There is no risk; the forwarder is read-only and harmless.
choices.rationale: The forwarder actively forwards mail.

questions.explanation: The forwarder keeps sending mail externally even after the rule is removed. Two mechanisms, two removals. The inbox rule and the mailbox-level forwarder are independent exfiltration paths.

questions.prompt: Recommendation: remove "AP Filter" (forwards *invoice* to attacker, deletes original). You list the user's rules and find: (a) "AP Filter" (malicious), (b) "Move newsletters to Reading folder" (months old, personal), (c) "Forward customer-success mail to my manager" (set up 2 days ago, in the compromise window). What do you do?

choices.label: Remove all three rules to be thorough.
choices.rationale: Acts beyond the Recommendation. The newsletter rule is legitimate; the manager-forward needs SOC review.

choices.label: Remove only "AP Filter" and do not mention the others.
choices.rationale: Half-right. The 2-day-old forward is in the compromise window and worth flagging to the SOC.

questions.explanation: Note the newsletter rule as legitimate. Reply to the SOC about the manager-forward (set within the compromise window). Document what was checked. Targeted action per the Recommendation. Ambiguous-in-window rules surface to the SOC. Documentation serves the next reader.

questions.prompt: You check ForwardingSmtpAddress: set to the attacker address, same as the removed rule. The Recommendation did not mention it. What do you do?

choices.label: Remove the forwarder yourself because it is obviously malicious.
choices.rationale: Acting on context the Recommendation did not include. The SOC owns the disposition.

choices: Reply to the SOC: "Mailbox-level ForwardingSmtpAddress also set to the attacker address.

choices.label: Stop the playbook until the SOC responds.
choices.rationale: Sessions and password are already addressed. The rest of the playbook continues in parallel.

questions.explanation: Should this be cleared?" Continue the playbook. Surface to the SOC rather than act unilaterally. Continue the playbook while waiting for the SOC's response.

questions.prompt: The Recommendation mentions removing a tenant-wide transport rule. What does the lesson say?
choices: Tenant-wide transport rules are senior territory.

choices.label: Remove the transport rule yourself.
choices.rationale: Tenant-wide is above the helpdesk ceiling.

choices.label: Ignore tenant-wide rules; they are not in scope.
choices.rationale: Ignoring is wrong; bumping is the action.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours and check if the transport rule is still there.
choices.rationale: Wrong delay; bump is the action.

questions.explanation: This is a tenant-wide-compromise indicator. Bump. Tenant-wide transport rules sit above the helpdesk ceiling. Bump to senior.

questions.prompt: A removed inbox rule had moved 500 emails to a hidden folder over the past three hours. What is the right framing?

choices.label: Delete the hidden folder and its contents.
choices.rationale: The mail may be the only record of what the attacker read; deletion destroys evidence.

choices: Note the folder and the volume so the customer can review what was there.

choices.label: Ignore the routed mail. Only the rule itself matters.
choices.rationale: The routed mail is part of the compromise impact.

choices.label: Forward all 500 emails to the SOC for review.
choices.rationale: The SOC does not ingest forwarded mail that way. Document the folder and volume.

questions.explanation: The routed mail is a potential exfiltration trail. The routed mail is a potential exfiltration trail. Document the folder and volume for the customer's record.

questions.prompt: The mailbox-level forwarder is set to an internal address that looks legitimate (a delegated mailbox in the same tenant). What do you do?

choices.label: Remove it because forwarders are always malicious.
choices.rationale: Many internal forwarders are legitimate delegation.

choices.label: Leave it because internal forwarders are always safe.
choices.rationale: Could be lateral attacker movement within the tenant.

choices: Bump rather than remove without context.

choices.label: Send a message to the delegated mailbox owner to ask.
choices.rationale: Senior coordinates customer-side comms during an active incident.

questions.explanation: Could be legitimate delegation; could be lateral movement. Ambiguous internal-delegation patterns escalate. The senior or SOC provides context before action.

questions.prompt: Which is the right post-removal verification check?

choices.label: Portal shows the action complete; nothing else needed.
choices.rationale: Action complete is not the full state check.

choices: Named inbox rules no longer exist;

choices.label: Ask the user to confirm they no longer have the rule.
choices.rationale: The platform state is the source of truth; users may not see the changes immediately.

choices.label: Wait 24 hours and check then.
choices.rationale: Wrong delay; verification is immediate.

questions.explanation: Forwarder fields are empty; Timeline shows no new rule-created or forwarder-set events post-remediation; documented in the Incident Report. Four checks: rules gone, forwarder fields empty, no new routing events, documented. Immediate verification catches silent failures.

questions.prompt: If the user has many unusual inbox rules that are not in the Recommendation, you should remove them all to be safe.
questions.explanation: Reply to the SOC with the list. Do not remove unilaterally. They may be legitimate user organisation or additional compromise artefacts the SOC should evaluate.

questions.prompt: Put the inbox-rule and forwarder removal procedure in order.
items: Pull the user's current inbox rules and compare against the Incident Report's Evidence.
Remove the malicious rules named in the Recommendation.
Check ForwardingSmtpAddress and ForwardingAddress; remove or surface to SOC.
Note where existing mail was routed (hidden folders) for the customer's record.
Verify: rules gone, forwarder fields empty, no new routing events.
questions.explanation: List, remove targeted rules, check forwarders, note routed mail, verify. The forwarder check is the step most often skipped.
